metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Open sandbox level with one stack, one queue, and one list.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Solve the basic Tower of Hanoi using three stacks.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Place the stack block, choose one operation, and run it. Only one action should happen: remove the top of A.

introduces: placing one structure block, selecting an operation, and executing it

messages.title: Place the stack block and choose an operation
messages.body: Look at the initial and goal boards first. The only difference is that the top value of A disappears. Drag structure A into the program, choose an operation for it, then press Play and watch what happens.

messages.title: One operation changes the stack, one only looks at it
messages.body: In this level, one available operation inspects the stack and the other one changes it. Compare the board after running and choose the operation that actually removes the top.

messages.title: Match the exact visible difference
messages.body: The goal board is missing exactly one value: the current top of A. Use the operation that removes that top value, then run again and observe the board.

introduces: placing one structure block, selecting an operation, and executing it

messages.title: Place the stack block and choose an operation
messages.body: Look at the initial and goal boards first. The only difference is that the top value of A disappears. Drag structure A into the program, choose an operation for it, then press Play and watch what happens.

messages.title: One operation changes the stack, one only looks at it
messages.body: In this level, one available operation inspects the stack and the other one changes it. Compare the board after running and choose the operation that actually removes the top.

messages.title: Match the exact visible difference
messages.body: The goal board is missing exactly one value: the current top of A. Use the operation that removes that top value, then run again and observe the board.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Use step-by-step execution to follow a two-transfer solution, then reset and try again if needed.

introduces: step-by-step execution and reset for debugging a short solution

messages.title: This level is for stepping through the program
messages.body: The solution is still short, but now it has more than one transfer. Use Step to see each change happen, and use Reset if you want to restart the board from the beginning.

messages.title: Watch the board after each step
messages.body: If the result is not right, inspect the board after each action. The goal is not only to finish, but also to understand which move changes the state.

messages.title: Repeat the same transfer rule carefully
messages.body: This level works by repeating the same move twice. Step through it and check whether each transfer leaves the board closer to the goal.

introduces: step-by-step execution and reset for debugging a short solution

messages.title: This level is for stepping through the program
messages.body: The solution is still short, but now it has more than one transfer. Use Step to see each change happen, and use Reset if you want to restart the board from the beginning.

messages.title: Watch the board after each step
messages.body: If the result is not right, inspect the board after each action. The goal is not only to finish, but also to understand which move changes the state.

messages.title: Repeat the same transfer rule carefully
messages.body: This level works by repeating the same move twice. Step through it and check whether each transfer leaves the board closer to the goal.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Practice comparing the initial and goal boards before writing anything. One value moves, but one value must stay where it is.

introduces: reading initial and goal states before planning a solution

messages.title: Read the target before touching the program
messages.body: This level is about planning first. Compare both boards and notice that not everything changes: one value must move, but another value on B must stay exactly where it already is.

messages.title: Not every visible value should move
messages.body: If B lost the value it already had, then the program changed more than the goal asks for. Match the target board carefully before running again.

messages.title: Solve the exact difference
messages.body: A good plan starts by asking what is different between the two boards. This level can be solved by changing only that exact difference.

introduces: reading initial and goal states before planning a solution

messages.title: Read the target before touching the program
messages.body: This level is about planning first. Compare both boards and notice that not everything changes: one value must move, but another value on B must stay exactly where it already is.

messages.title: Not every visible value should move
messages.body: If B lost the value it already had, then the program changed more than the goal asks for. Match the target board carefully before running again.

messages.title: Solve the exact difference
messages.body: A good plan starts by asking what is different between the two boards. This level can be solved by changing only that exact difference.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: A short autonomous checkpoint: compare the boards, choose the correct move order, and solve the full mini challenge by yourself.

introduces: autonomous resolution of a short challenge using the full editor flow

messages.title: Time for a small independent challenge
messages.body: This level combines the workflow from the previous onboarding levels. Read both boards, decide the correct order of moves, then solve it without step-by-step guidance.

messages.title: Order matters now
messages.body: The target stack C must receive values in the right order. If the result is reversed, check which transfer should happen first.

messages.title: Use the whole routine you learned
messages.body: Compare, plan, run, and correct. This checkpoint is not introducing a new tool; it is checking that you can use the editor flow on your own.

introduces: autonomous resolution of a short challenge using the full editor flow

messages.title: Time for a small independent challenge
messages.body: This level combines the workflow from the previous onboarding levels. Read both boards, decide the correct order of moves, then solve it without step-by-step guidance.

messages.title: Order matters now
messages.body: The target stack C must receive values in the right order. If the result is reversed, check which transfer should happen first.

messages.title: Use the whole routine you learned
messages.body: Compare, plan, run, and correct. This checkpoint is not introducing a new tool; it is checking that you can use the editor flow on your own.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Learn stack basics: use POP on A and PUSH into B to move the top element.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Practice repetition: empty A with POP and build B from scratch using PUSH only.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Mix default data structures: reverse queue Q using stack S as auxiliary memory.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Learn list edge operations: trim exactly one item from each side of L.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Create a helper function (for example moveOne) and reuse it to transfer A into B.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Define a custom function and reuse it to move values from stack A into queue Q.

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Create a custom type (example: Box), then solve the stack transfer using your own model objects if you want.

title: Campaign 08 · Integrate Custom Type + Default DS

metadata.author: Core Team
metadata.description: Final challenge: solve with default structures and optionally model helper data using a custom type.
